title: Dog Seizure Lasting 2 Minutes: Triage Guide & Emergency Steps
meta_desc: If your dog had a seizure lasting 2 minutes, this is a high-priority emergency. Learn what to do next and how to help your vet with the diagnosis.
content_html:
What is a Canine Seizure?

A seizure is a temporary, involuntary disturbance of normal brain function usually accompanied by uncontrollable muscle activity. When a seizure lasts for two minutes, it indicates a significant episode of electrical misfiring in the brain. During this time, your dog may lose consciousness, collapse, paddle their legs, or lose control of their bladder. The period immediately following the seizure, known as the post-ictal phase, often involves confusion, pacing, or temporary blindness.

Is a 2-Minute Seizure an Emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, a seizure lasting two minutes is considered a medical emergency. While a single, very brief seizure (under 60 seconds) is concerning, a two-minute duration increases the risk of hyperthermia (overheating) and potential brain damage. If your dog has multiple seizures in a 24-hour period or if the seizure does not stop, it is a life-threatening situation known as status epilepticus. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately for stabilization and diagnostic testing.

How Photos and Video Help Triage

If it is safe to do so, capturing a video of the event is one of the most powerful tools you can provide to a veterinary team. Many conditions, such as fainting (syncope) or severe tremors, can look like seizures to the untrained eye. A video allows the vet to observe the specific type of movement, the duration, and the level of consciousness. This visual evidence helps them differentiate between neurological and cardiovascular issues, leading to a faster and more accurate treatment plan.

faqs.question: What should I do while my dog is seizing?
faqs.answer: Keep your hands away from their mouth to avoid accidental bites. Clear away furniture to prevent injury, time the seizure, and keep the environment quiet and dark.

faqs.question: Will my dog swallow their tongue?
faqs.answer: No, dogs do not swallow their tongues during seizures. Never put your fingers or any objects into their mouth, as this can cause injury to both you and your pet.

faqs.question: Can I wait to see if it happens again before calling a vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because the seizure lasted two minutes, your dog needs an immediate evaluation to check their vitals and ensure they do not enter a cycle of cluster seizures.
